author: @DiyEcoProjects
text: 5:44 Hi there, wow the speed of that thing, realy cool !!!. Just thinkng, i wonder if you had one of your awesome "serpant coills" from 1869 on the outside disc and the planet gears had some magnets embeded in them somehow ~ ~ ~ would that make a good generator. If not then perhaps a disc of a serpant coil, propped up against the gears (with magnets inside) to minimise the gap. Thank you for your wonderful creations, hope things are good, All the best, Kieron

But  the past few days I have been lookinging into all my old fishing reels and how some I have spin 5.5  the original turn.  And so have been looking at ways to use old fishing reels to increase the RPM to increase voltage etc etc...  Then you uploaded this video.  Life is GOOD.

replies.author: @DiyEcoProjects

author: @l0I0I0I0
text: The higher the voltage, the less copper needed as well.

author: @l0I0I0I0
text:
More volts?  Easy.
Hook up a printer and print as many volts as you want.

author: @trevorgoddard2278
text:
There is a much more efficient way of using planetary gears which can provide high ratios with only two sets of planetary gears.
The split-ring compound planetary epicyclic gear system.  The calculation of ratios is more complex, but ratios up to hundreds to one are possible in 3D printed parts.
There are several YouTube videos on the subject, but they are all using them for motor down gearing with much higher ratios than you would want.

author: @MinexZ1
text: Hello from France, i discovered recently your chanel. Thank you for your videos.. Do you know the efficiency of this type of this  multiplicator wih an industrial type ?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: A good, professionally machined, straight cut "spur" gear will usually have a mechanical efficiency of 98-99%. Since the planetary system involves several gears, I would guess 95% per layer. With proper lubrication of course.

author: @banana1618
text:
another fascinating video - it seems an optimal system is where there is a gearing system that allows a propeller to spin with a low wind (low speed and low torque) and generate minimal voltage but then, when the wind gets stronger, to increase the gearing automatically so the electricity production increases exponentially ie a variable gearing system... If that makes sense. 
You are on a roll, Robert - great ideas and implementation. Seems having a flock of 3D printers and tickercad might be helping somewhat!

replies.author: @EvrttGrn
replies.text: That sounds like a cvt system similar to what newer go-karts use using 2 variable pulleys, weights, springs and a belt in between.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: like a variable transmission?

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206

For now.

author: @robertsmith4681
text: It would be interesting to strap a force meter to the crank and see how much more "effort" is required as you add stacks. If one was to use something like a Fresnel lens to heat up a lump of metal, stick a Stirling engine on said hot of lump metal, use that to spin a heavy-ish flywheel and strap the flywheel to such a stack of gears that runs a generator, could one get to something that runs for useful amounts of time <and> produces useful amounts of current, enough to run something along the lines of a trickle charger ... ?
